what_it_does: This file is a captured noticeboard item that records a planned, behavior-preserving refactor to unify cc_board.traverse with the H1.2 structural core (_edges_out) when the traversal layer is next modified.
observations: It is a ledger item of type 'idea' in state 'captured' sourced from claude_code.
It references two architectural layers: cc_board.traverse (H1.1, eager-resolve forward) and _edges_out (H1.2, structural read).
It cites a fork (ch-8djrpmsl) that proposed the refactor.
It explicitly guards the change by requiring heart_resolution_acceptance (11/11) and cc_board_acceptance (26/26) to stay green.
It labels the work as cosmetic and not urgent, but tracked so it is not deferred.
standouts: The core proposal is to collapse two overlapping link-reading implementations into a single structural core while preserving traverse's always-hydrate contract.
It ties the refactor to concrete acceptance-test invariants rather than a schedule.
It is framed as 'no-deferral' despite being non-urgent.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, state, title, author_session, timestamps, history, and links.
Uses internal board:// addressing for item references.
Includes acceptance-test counts as a guard condition.
concerns: The title is long and embeds implementation specifics.
The content assumes familiarity with H1.1/H1.2, cc_board, and fork identifiers not defined in the file.
There is no explicit owner or trigger definition for 'when the traversal layer is next touched'.
It claims 'no-deferral' but remains in 'captured' state without a scheduled action.
notes: A captured refactor note to merge cc_board.traverse with the _edges_out structural core, preserving behavior and guarded by existing acceptance tests.
questions: What are the exact behavioral differences between the H1.1 traverse and H1.2 _edges_out implementations?
What do heart_resolution_acceptance and cc_board_acceptance specifically cover?
What determines when the traversal layer is 'next touched'?
Who is responsible for executing this refactor?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to track a behavior-preserving unification refactor, and the file does exactly that as a captured idea.
intention_signals: It is trying to ensure a known overlap between two traversal implementations is not forgotten, by recording a safe, test-guarded refactor opportunity for the next time that layer is edited.
novelty: It uses a noticeboard item as a persistent, non-urgent refactor ticket with explicit acceptance-test invariants and a promoted-from link.
contribution: It contributes a precise, test-guarded refactor intent and architectural rationale (the relationship between H1.1 and H1.2) that a fused system could retain as a work item.
summary_for_embedding: Captured noticeboard idea proposing to unify cc_board.traverse (H1.1 eager-resolve forward) onto the _edges_out structural core (H1.2) in a behavior-preserving refactor guarded by acceptance tests when the traversal layer is next modified.
intention_for_embedding: Record a non-urgent but non-deferred refactor opportunity to eliminate overlap between traversal and structural link-reading code.
